title: Child Safeguarding — Recognition
oneLiner: Recognising physical, sexual, emotional abuse or neglect in children; every health professional has a duty to act to protect the child.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Non-accidental head injury (NAHI) / abusive head trauma**
  - *Single finding that says "this is the killer":* Apnoeic episode, bulging fontanelle, retinal haemorrhage (bilateral, multi-layered) – <1yr old, no clear accidental mechanism.
- **Intra-abdominal injury (blunt force)**
  - *Single finding that says "this is the killer":* Unexplained abdominal bruising, distension, + hypovolaemic shock – child <4yrs, no history of high-energy trauma.
- **Severe neglect / failure to thrive (FTT) with acute decompensation**
  - *Single finding that says "this is the killer":* Hypothermia, bradycardia, hypoglycaemia in an infant with no organic cause – profound deprivation.
- **Mimic that kills if treated as abuse:** *Meningococcaemia* – purpuric rash + shock; *Osteogenesis imperfecta* – multiple fractures with blue sclerae; *Bleeding disorder* – large bruises, haemarthroses, family history.

---
sections.Presentation:
- **Physical abuse:**
  - Bruises – on non-mobile infant (not over bony prominences), patterned (belt, hand, teeth), multiple in different stages
  - Burns – cigarette, immersion (stocking/glove distribution), contact (iron/grill)
  - Fractures – femur or humerus in <1yr, multiple ribs, metaphyseal chip fractures
  - Oral injuries – torn frenulum (controversial but suspect if isolated + no explanation), dental trauma
- **Neglect:**
  - Poor weight gain, dirty/clothes inappropriate, missed appointments, constant hunger, untreated dental/medical conditions
- **Emotional abuse:**
  - Withdrawn, extreme aggression, delayed speech, indiscriminate attachment
- **Sexual abuse:**
  - Genital/anal bruising, discharge, STI, behavioural change (sexualised play, regression)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Full child undressed (do in warm room, chaperone) – inspect all skin surfaces, head to toe
  - Growth centiles (falling off = red flag)
  - Developmental assessment (delay may be neglect signal)

---
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  1. Accidental injury (matching history + mechanism, mobile child, typical site: shins, elbows)
  2. Non-accidental injury (discrepant history, pattern, location inconsistent with age)
  3. Bleeding disorder (spontaneous bruises, epistaxis, menorrhagia, family history – check coagulation)
  4. Osteogenesis imperfecta (multiple fractures, blue sclerae, deafness, family history)
  5. Cultural practices (coining/cupping – ask about traditional medicine – symmetrical circular bruises)
  6. Brittle bone disease vs NAI – skeletal survey + ophthalmology + genetics consult
- **Splitter:** “Bruises over bony prominences (shins, forehead) in a mobile child → accidental; bruises on torso, neck, ear, buttocks in an infant → strongly suspect non-accidental”

---
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Body map / photography (consent from guardian – if refused, document and proceed if child protection concern)
  - Full set of observations with PEWS (paediatric early warning score)
  - Urine dip for blood if abdominal injury suspected
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, clotting screen (PT/APTT), von Willebrand factor (if bleeding history)
  - LFTs/amylase (pancreatic injury), bone profile (metabolic bone disease)
  - Skeletal survey (<2yrs – whole body XR series including oblique ribs; follow NICE guidelines)
  - CT head (if suspected NAHI – urgent)
  - Toxicology screen if altered consciousness
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Skeletal survey (under 2yrs); CT head ± MRI (for NAHI)
  - **Second-line:** Bone scan (if older child and skeletal survey negative but high suspicion)
- **Special tests:**
  - Ophthalmology review (dilated fundoscopy – retinal haemorrhages)
  - Genital/forensic examination (by specialist paediatrician)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - NICE guideline CG89 – suspicious injury features: no explanation, inconsistency, delay, history of abuse/family violence
  - Modified Child Abuse Potential Inventory (not done clinically – use clinical judgment + local safeguarding tool)
- **Never-miss test:** **Skeletal survey in any child <2yrs with unexplained injury** – omission misses occult fractures that are pathognomonic of abuse (e.g., rib, metaphyseal).

---
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Stabilise the child (ABC) – do not delay life-saving treatment even if safeguarding concern
  - If suspected abuse, remove from immediate risk – separate child from suspected perpetrator
  - Inform paediatric registrar + named doctor/nurse for safeguarding
  - Do not confront parents – say “we need to understand why this happened” – non-accusatory language
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Complete all investigations (imaging, bloods, ophthalmology) within 24h
  - Document injuries on body map – date, time, staff member, witness signature
  - Multidisciplinary team huddle (paediatrics, safeguarding, social work, police)
  - Notify local authority children’s social care (if threshold met)
  - Safeguarding strategy meeting / Section 47 enquiry
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Child protection plan (conference within 15 working days)
  - Paediatric follow-up for growth, development, trauma sequelae
  - Referral to child and adolescent mental health services (CAMHS) if emotional abuse
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids if nil-by-mouth for surgery/imaging – 0.45% NaCl + 5% glucose if <1yr, 0.9% NaCl if >1yr (rate per Holliday-Segar)
  - VTE prophylaxis – not routine in children; consider if >12yr + immobilised (enoxaparin – check local weight-based dosing)
  - Diet – NBM until plan clear (esp. if intra-abdominal injury suspected)
  - Observations – 4-hourly minimum; increased to 1-2 hourly if acute head/abdo injury or PEWS trigger

---
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- *No drug is specific to recognition; if analgesia needed for injuries:*
  - Paracetamol 15 mg/kg PO/IV (max 60 mg/kg/day) – use as first-line
  - Ibuprofen 5-10 mg/kg PO (max 30 mg/kg/day) – avoid if renal impairment, hypovolaemia, or <3mo
  - Morphine (if severe pain): 0.1-0.2 mg/kg IV/SC – [check local policy for max single dose]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs caution in renal impairment, hypovolaemia, bleeding risk (may mask clinical signs of intra-abdominal bleeding – document decision carefully)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Paracetamol – reduce dose in hepatic impairment (max 40-60 mg/kg/day)
  - Morphine – avoid in renal failure (accumulation of metabolite)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Overdose of paracetamol in a small child – use age/formulation charts. In suspected abuse, any drug error adds to safeguarding concerns.

---
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Subdural haematoma – rising ICP, seizures, herniation
  - Intra-abdominal haemorrhage – hypovolaemic shock
  - Death from untreated injury or neglect (sepsis, hypothermia)
- **Chronic:**
  - Developmental delay, permanent neurological deficits (blindness, cognitive impairment)
  - PTSD, attachment disorder, long-term mental health problems
  - Recurrence of abuse if no intervention

---
sections.Prognosis:
- Early recognition + removal to safe environment improves outcomes; 20-30% of severe physical abuse survivors have permanent disability. Neglect carries high long-term morbidity (cognitive deficits, poor school performance). Mortality is highest in infants <6mo with head injury.

---
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always examine the entire child** (undress, in good light, chaperone) – you can miss chest/back bruising if just auscultate through clothing.
- **Use a body diagram** to record every lesion – take photographs with consent (or without if parent refuses and child at risk – document reasoning).
- **Check siblings** – ask “are there other children at home?” Inform social care about siblings even if they appear well.

---
sections.Pitfalls (Errors to avoid):
- **Don’t accept a vague or changing history** – “she fell off the sofa” is insufficient for a femur fracture in a 4-month-old. Escalate for senior review.
- **Don’t blame parents in front of the child** – remain non-judgmental; you may need them to cooperate. You can be honest: “this injury needs an explanation we can’t find yet”.
- **Don’t delay referral because you’re uncertain** – if you have “reasonable cause to suspect”, you must refer to children’s social care (Working Together 2018). Document your concerns and discussion.

---
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
*Note: Children with suspected abuse are rarely discharged without safeguarding plan. Discharge only if low suspicion + clear follow-up.*
- **Return if:** New bruising, lethargy, inconsolable crying, vomiting (esp. head injury), refusal to feed, parental behaviour changes (agitation, attempting to leave against advice).
- **Follow-up:** Paediatric outpatients within 48h; safeguarding team contact family in 24h. Ensure GP is informed of plan.

---
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call paediatric registrar immediately if:** Child <1yr with any unexplained fracture or head injury; retinal haemorrhage; hypovolaemic shock with abdominal bruising; parent attempts to remove child from hospital against advice.
- **Call safeguarding named professional (bleep or call)** for all red flag cases before child leaves the department.
- **ICU criteria:** GCS ≤8, persistent apnoea, refractory shock, status epilepticus (paediatric intensive care referral).
- **NEWS2 / PEWS:** Use age-specific PEWS – trigger threshold is total score ≥4 (local variant) – escalate per trust policy. In infants <1yr, any single parameter outside normal range (e.g., HR >180, RR >60) warrants senior review.

rawMarkdown:
## Child Safeguarding — Recognition

**One-liner:** Recognising physical, sexual, emotional abuse or neglect in children; every health professional has a duty to act to protect the child.

---

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Non-accidental head injury (NAHI) / abusive head trauma**
  - *Single finding that says "this is the killer":* Apnoeic episode, bulging fontanelle, retinal haemorrhage (bilateral, multi-layered) – <1yr old, no clear accidental mechanism.
- **Intra-abdominal injury (blunt force)**
  - *Single finding that says "this is the killer":* Unexplained abdominal bruising, distension, + hypovolaemic shock – child <4yrs, no history of high-energy trauma.
- **Severe neglect / failure to thrive (FTT) with acute decompensation**
  - *Single finding that says "this is the killer":* Hypothermia, bradycardia, hypoglycaemia in an infant with no organic cause – profound deprivation.
- **Mimic that kills if treated as abuse:** *Meningococcaemia* – purpuric rash + shock; *Osteogenesis imperfecta* – multiple fractures with blue sclerae; *Bleeding disorder* – large bruises, haemarthroses, family history.

---

### Presentation

- **Physical abuse:**
  - Bruises – on non-mobile infant (not over bony prominences), patterned (belt, hand, teeth), multiple in different stages
  - Burns – cigarette, immersion (stocking/glove distribution), contact (iron/grill)
  - Fractures – femur or humerus in <1yr, multiple ribs, metaphyseal chip fractures
  - Oral injuries – torn frenulum (controversial but suspect if isolated + no explanation), dental trauma
- **Neglect:**
  - Poor weight gain, dirty/clothes inappropriate, missed appointments, constant hunger, untreated dental/medical conditions
- **Emotional abuse:**
  - Withdrawn, extreme aggression, delayed speech, indiscriminate attachment
- **Sexual abuse:**
  - Genital/anal bruising, discharge, STI, behavioural change (sexualised play, regression)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Full child undressed (do in warm room, chaperone) – inspect all skin surfaces, head to toe
  - Growth centiles (falling off = red flag)
  - Developmental assessment (delay may be neglect signal)

---

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely → less likely:**
  1. Accidental injury (matching history + mechanism, mobile child, typical site: shins, elbows)
  2. Non-accidental injury (discrepant history, pattern, location inconsistent with age)
  3. Bleeding disorder (spontaneous bruises, epistaxis, menorrhagia, family history – check coagulation)
  4. Osteogenesis imperfecta (multiple fractures, blue sclerae, deafness, family history)
  5. Cultural practices (coining/cupping – ask about traditional medicine – symmetrical circular bruises)
  6. Brittle bone disease vs NAI – skeletal survey + ophthalmology + genetics consult
- **Splitter:** “Bruises over bony prominences (shins, forehead) in a mobile child → accidental; bruises on torso, neck, ear, buttocks in an infant → strongly suspect non-accidental”

---

### Investigations

- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Body map / photography (consent from guardian – if refused, document and proceed if child protection concern)
  - Full set of observations with PEWS (paediatric early warning score)
  - Urine dip for blood if abdominal injury suspected
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, clotting screen (PT/APTT), von Willebrand factor (if bleeding history)
  - LFTs/amylase (pancreatic injury), bone profile (metabolic bone disease)
  - Skeletal survey (<2yrs – whole body XR series including oblique ribs; follow NICE guidelines)
  - CT head (if suspected NAHI – urgent)
  - Toxicology screen if altered consciousness
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Skeletal survey (under 2yrs); CT head ± MRI (for NAHI)
  - **Second-line:** Bone scan (if older child and skeletal survey negative but high suspicion)
- **Special tests:**
  - Ophthalmology review (dilated fundoscopy – retinal haemorrhages)
  - Genital/forensic examination (by specialist paediatrician)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - NICE guideline CG89 – suspicious injury features: no explanation, inconsistency, delay, history of abuse/family violence
  - Modified Child Abuse Potential Inventory (not done clinically – use clinical judgment + local safeguarding tool)
- **Never-miss test:** **Skeletal survey in any child <2yrs with unexplained injury** – omission misses occult fractures that are pathognomonic of abuse (e.g., rib, metaphyseal).

---

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Stabilise the child (ABC) – do not delay life-saving treatment even if safeguarding concern
  - If suspected abuse, remove from immediate risk – separate child from suspected perpetrator
  - Inform paediatric registrar + named doctor/nurse for safeguarding
  - Do not confront parents – say “we need to understand why this happened” – non-accusatory language
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Complete all investigations (imaging, bloods, ophthalmology) within 24h
  - Document injuries on body map – date, time, staff member, witness signature
  - Multidisciplinary team huddle (paediatrics, safeguarding, social work, police)
  - Notify local authority children’s social care (if threshold met)
  - Safeguarding strategy meeting / Section 47 enquiry
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Child protection plan (conference within 15 working days)
  - Paediatric follow-up for growth, development, trauma sequelae
  - Referral to child and adolescent mental health services (CAMHS) if emotional abuse
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids if nil-by-mouth for surgery/imaging – 0.45% NaCl + 5% glucose if <1yr, 0.9% NaCl if >1yr (rate per Holliday-Segar)
  - VTE prophylaxis – not routine in children; consider if >12yr + immobilised (enoxaparin – check local weight-based dosing)
  - Diet – NBM until plan clear (esp. if intra-abdominal injury suspected)
  - Observations – 4-hourly minimum; increased to 1-2 hourly if acute head/abdo injury or PEWS trigger

---

### Drugs & Doses

- *No drug is specific to recognition; if analgesia needed for injuries:*
  - Paracetamol 15 mg/kg PO/IV (max 60 mg/kg/day) – use as first-line
  - Ibuprofen 5-10 mg/kg PO (max 30 mg/kg/day) – avoid if renal impairment, hypovolaemia, or <3mo
  - Morphine (if severe pain): 0.1-0.2 mg/kg IV/SC – [check local policy for max single dose]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs caution in renal impairment, hypovolaemia, bleeding risk (may mask clinical signs of intra-abdominal bleeding – document decision carefully)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Paracetamol – reduce dose in hepatic impairment (max 40-60 mg/kg/day)
  - Morphine – avoid in renal failure (accumulation of metabolite)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Overdose of paracetamol in a small child – use age/formulation charts. In suspected abuse, any drug error adds to safeguarding concerns.

---

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Subdural haematoma – rising ICP, seizures, herniation
  - Intra-abdominal haemorrhage – hypovolaemic shock
  - Death from untreated injury or neglect (sepsis, hypothermia)
- **Chronic:**
  - Developmental delay, permanent neurological deficits (blindness, cognitive impairment)
  - PTSD, attachment disorder, long-term mental health problems
  - Recurrence of abuse if no intervention

---

### Prognosis

- Early recognition + removal to safe environment improves outcomes; 20-30% of severe physical abuse survivors have permanent disability. Neglect carries high long-term morbidity (cognitive deficits, poor school performance). Mortality is highest in infants <6mo with head injury.

---

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always examine the entire child** (undress, in good light, chaperone) – you can miss chest/back bruising if just auscultate through clothing.
- **Use a body diagram** to record every lesion – take photographs with consent (or without if parent refuses and child at risk – document reasoning).
- **Check siblings** – ask “are there other children at home?” Inform social care about siblings even if they appear well.

---

### Pitfalls (Errors to avoid)

- **Don’t accept a vague or changing history** – “she fell off the sofa” is insufficient for a femur fracture in a 4-month-old. Escalate for senior review.
- **Don’t blame parents in front of the child** – remain non-judgmental; you may need them to cooperate. You can be honest: “this injury needs an explanation we can’t find yet”.
- **Don’t delay referral because you’re uncertain** – if you have “reasonable cause to suspect”, you must refer to children’s social care (Working Together 2018). Document your concerns and discussion.

---

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

*Note: Children with suspected abuse are rarely discharged without safeguarding plan. Discharge only if low suspicion + clear follow-up.*
- **Return if:** New bruising, lethargy, inconsolable crying, vomiting (esp. head injury), refusal to feed, parental behaviour changes (agitation, attempting to leave against advice).
- **Follow-up:** Paediatric outpatients within 48h; safeguarding team contact family in 24h. Ensure GP is informed of plan.

---

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call paediatric registrar immediately if:** Child <1yr with any unexplained fracture or head injury; retinal haemorrhage; hypovolaemic shock with abdominal bruising; parent attempts to remove child from hospital against advice.
- **Call safeguarding named professional (bleep or call)** for all red flag cases before child leaves the department.
- **ICU criteria:** GCS ≤8, persistent apnoea, refractory shock, status epilepticus (paediatric intensive care referral).
- **NEWS2 / PEWS:** Use age-specific PEWS – trigger threshold is total score ≥4 (local variant) – escalate per trust policy. In infants <1yr, any single parameter outside normal range (e.g., HR >180, RR >60) warrants senior review.
